text: Robotic preperitoneal repair of a large spagalian hernia in the setting of a large abdominal aortic aneurysm. Disclosures include intuitive, barred, and gore.

The patient is an eighty-two-year-old male with a large, left-sided, incarcerated spagalian hernia that was causing him significant discomfort. His past medical history was significant for non-insulin-dependent diabetes, stage three chronic kidney disease, and atrial fibrillation for which he took Xarelto. His surgical history was significant for an open right hemicolectomy for a benign polyp, as well as an endovascular aneurysm repair for an eight-centimeter abdominal aortic aneurysm. that had a known type II endoleak. He also had a remote smoking history and was a daily drinker. These are images showing the resulting changes in his abdominal wall contour caused by this hernia. His preoperative CT shows the previous extensive EVAR stent as well as a large left-sided Spigalian hernia containing loops of bowel without evidence of bowel compromise.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using a Veress needle in the left upper quadrant, followed by the insertion of a 12mm trocar with the OptiView technique. An 8mm trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant and two in the right mid-abdomen. Extended lysis of adhesions is performed to expose the left Spigalian hernia defect, with the sigmoid colon adherent to the hernia sac. The peritoneum in the left lower quadrant is incised to enter the preperitoneal plane, dividing the preperitoneal attachments and reducing the hernia sac. The large abdominal aortic aneurysm is visible, pulsating in the foreground, complicating visualization and dissection.

An assistant port is used to retract the redundant hernia sac and its contents, allowing for division of the ventral attachments. The surgeon develops the pre-peritoneal space to fit the anticipated mesh size, carefully identifying and protecting the spermatic cord and inferior epigastric vessels. After partially closing the defect with an O V-lock suture, a stab incision is made at the center of the defect, and a seven French flat JP drain is placed between the external and internal oblique muscles in the space previously occupied by the hernia sac.

The surgeon measures the defect at three by five centimeters and closes it transversely with an OV-lock suture. A fifteen-centimeter circular Ventralite ST echo mesh is introduced and unrolled. A suture passer is used to pull up on the central cord, and the structural mesh balloon is inflated to ensure adequate preperitoneal space. The mesh is secured with TuoVigril sutures in multiple locations, avoiding the triangle of pain and nerve trajectories. The positioning balloon is removed, and the mesh lies flat against the abdominal wall. The peritoneal flap is closed with a V-lock suture.
The surgeon oversews any remaining peritoneal defects, completing the repair of the hernia.

His postoperative course was prolonged, secondary to development of a large preperitoneal hematoma after resuming his Xarelto, highlighted by the red star on this representative cut of a postoperative CT. He also struggled with insomnia and delirium, resulting in early inadvertent removal of his JP drain and subsequent intramuscular seroma formation highlighted by the yellow star. Finally, he suffered a massive upper GI bleed from a duodenal ulcer that required multiple rounds of transfusion as well as multiple endoscopic interventions to control. This also occurred once his Xarelto was again resumed. Given his recurrent bleeding complications, it was deemed that the risks outweighed the benefits, and his Xarelta was stopped. Despite his prolonged course, he recovered fully from his complications. Watchful waiting was pursued for his hematoma and seroma, and interventional radiology is planning angioembolization for the type II endoleak.
